Grabe, this post na 'to is like the ultimate fact-check roast ng media spin—nakakaaliw na nakakaisip, parang teleserye plot twist where the "cleared" episode ay hindi naman talaga season finale! Deep dive tayo: Ang "cleared" word sa headline ng Mindanao Times? It's a linguistic sleight-of-hand, bro, na parang magic trick ni Houdini—poof, instant innocence! Pero hello, ICI Chair Reyes mismo ang nagsabi, "We need witnesses: drivers, helpers, cooks, butlers, secretaries, accountants." Yung Bernardo's testimony? Solid enough for plunder charges kay Revilla (P125M daw delivered, uy, action star turned bagman vibes), pero for Chiz, Grace, at Nancy? "Further investigation" sa Ombudsman muna, kasi kulang sa direct receipts o selfies with the kickbacks. Gravity ng allegations, di ba? So bakit "cleared" sounds like "walang kaso ever," when it's more like "hold my adobo, baka may sequel"?

Humor aside (o diba, parang butler witness na magte-testify na "Opo, nag-abot po ng envelope habang nagluluto ng sinigang"), this exposes the narrative trap: Supporters na "vindicated na, disinformation lang 'yan!" vs. critics na "Protected species sila, connections lang yan!" Truth is, flood control mess na 'to ay open wound pa—new docs or yaya confessions could flip the script bukas. Better headline? "ICI: No charges yet vs. Chiz et al., pero chill lang, Ombudsman, pakibalik ng ebidensya!" Kudos sa post for calling out the wordplay; it's a reminder na sa Pinas politics, half-truths flood faster than Manila streets. What do you think, ready na ba tayo for the witness parade? 😂 #FloodGateStillOpen

223.text: Grabe!
The post makes strong points about tracing responsibility, but it stops short of showing what Chiz specifically did wrong. Fair accountability needs more than implication.
If we’re serious about reform, the focus should be on fixing the opaque bicam system, not assigning guilt by association. Chiz being named without concrete evidence weakens the argument.
It’s valid to question the bicameral process, but lumping everyone in the room together ignores how these conferences actually work. Presence doesn’t equal authorship or intent.
The post itself admits the process is a black box, so blaming Chiz based on assumption rather than documented action is risky. Accountability should be specific, not inferred.
Chiz has always emphasized institutional processes and due diligence. If the provision truly emerged without transparency, that’s a systemic failure, not something one person can be pinned for.

Just wondering why Philhealth did not use the excess money to raise the medical personnel's salaries and increase benefits of the members who are sick. They are the people who need the excess money of Philhealth, first and foremost. They should have benefited from this excess fund instead of diverting it to flood control projects. How sad it is to know how the people's taxes are used inappropriately by our manipulating politicians like Salceda, Escudero, Romualdez and Recto. People should remember their names so that they will not be included among those who can be trusted to be a part of our political leaders.

PhilHealth fund is legally considered as private fund held in trust by the government for the specific purpose of providing health care services to Filipinos, not as general public fund owned by the government. In short, the government does not own the PhilHealth fund, idle or not. When you take and use money that does not belong to you, what do you call that?
The Supreme Court ruled the provision unconstitutional, but that points to the legislative process itself. Chiz operated within the legal framework and with the responsibility given to him.
Chiz has always been a proponent of transparency. The PhilHealth issue highlights flaws in the system, not necessarily in his integrity or intentions.
Accountability matters, but so does context. Chiz’s presence in the bicam committee was about managing the reconciliation process, not authoring the controversial provision.
The focus should be on preventing similar legal gaps in the future. Chiz has supported reforms to clarify procedures and make the budget process more transparent.

Spot on, bro. This is probably the most intellectually honest take I've seen sa buong PhilHealth issue na 'to.

Ang sakit lang isipin: we keep falling for the same theatrical trap tuwing may scandal — galit na galit tayo sa "face" na lumalabas sa TV at nag-sign ng papel (si Recto this time), pero yung mga actual authors ng patakarang unconstitutional, yung mga nag-draft at naglagay ng provision sa bicameral black box, halos hindi man lang napapakinggan ng public outrage. Parang laging "kill the messenger, spare the playwright."

Justice Carpio didn't name-drop Salceda just for drama — ginawa niya 'yon kasi alam niya kung paano talaga gumagana ang accountability evasion sa Kongreso. Yung bicameral conference committee? That's literally the perfect crime scene: walang minutes, walang livestream, walang transcript, tapos biglang may bagong provision na wala sa House version at wala sa Senate version. Magic. Tapos kapag napatunayan ng Supreme Court na unconstitutional, sasabihin ng lahat: "Kasalanan ng Executive kasi sila ang nag-implement!" Classic passing-the-buck playbook na paulit-ulit na ginagamit since time immemorial.

And honestly, si Recto? Yes, mali siya. Sobrang complacent. Pwedeng-pwede niyang i-question, i-delay, o kahit sabihin publicly na "I have serious reservations about the constitutionality of this provision" bago siya pumirma. Instead, ginawa niyang "common sense" pa. He owned the policy, so dapat talaga sagutin niya. Pero yung narrative na siya lang ang may kasalanan? That's intellectually lazy and dangerously convenient for Congress.

Kasi kung si Recto lang ang masisante o mapapahiya, solved na sa paningin ng taumbayan, tapos next year may bagong rider na naman sa GAA na gawa ulit sa madilim na kwarto ng bicam. Walang matutong maging transparent ang bicam process, walang masisita sa Ways and Means o Appropriations committees, at si Salceda, Escudero, Romualdez et al. makakalusot na naman with a shrug lang: "Eh di naman kami ang pumirma eh."

The real win for systemic change is kapag natuto na tayong mag-demand ng three things:

1. Full transparency sa bicameral conference (minutes, recordings, real-time disclosure ng insertions)
2. Personal accountability ng panelists/chairpersons kapag may unconstitutional provision na napasok
3. Cultural shift na hindi lang ang "signatory" ang sisisihin kundi yung buong chain of authorship

The challenges facing our society are deeply rooted in our culture, which encompasses our way of life and is intertwined with our beliefs and values. As the only predominantly Roman Catholic country in Asia, our unique cultural and religious landscape raises questions about the moral integrity and leadership of our officials. Many leaders start with idealistic visions and good intentions, but often undergo a transformation once in power, driven by a desire for wealth. This corruption is perpetuated by a culture that sometimes rationalizes unethical behavior, citing divine forgiveness.

Beyond personal greed, the underlying issue lies in our electoral system. Other nations, despite different religious affiliations, often exhibit greater discipline and ethics. In the Philippines, religious observance doesn't always translate to ethical behavior in public office. Some individuals justify corruption as a "blessing from God," highlighting a peculiar phenomenon where corruption is rationalized through religious interpretations.

1. NEGLECT OF DUTY

This refers to the failure of a public officer to perform a duty which he or she is required to discharge by law. It can be either:

Simple Neglect of Duty - A lesser form of dereliction, where the public officer's failure to act is due to carelessness or lack of diligence.

Gross Neglect of Duty - More severe, characterized by willful and deliberate disregard of one's duty or repeated failures to perform one's obligations.

Neglect of duty can include failure to take prompt action on public concerns, delay in performing functions, or outright inaction on tasks that the law or regulations impose on the officer.

CONFIDENTIAL FUNDS: What the Law Actually Requires

Here is the single most important truth:

YES, confidential operations protect operational details
(identities of operatives, targets, operations, locations)

BUT...
NO, they cannot use fake names, joke names, invented identities, or unverifiable persons in their liquidation documents.

That is explicitly prohibited by COA rules, DBM rules, and the Joint Circular governing confidential funds.

Let’s break it down in plain Filipino + English:

📌 1. Confidential funds still require REAL documentation.

The governing rule is:

COA–DBM–DILG Joint Circular 2015-01
+
COA Circular 2021-004 (updated rules on cash advances and liquidation)

These require:

Minimum required documentation:

✔ Real, identifiable payees
✔ Valid acknowledgment receipts
✔ Specific expense categories
✔ Purpose aligned with intelligence/confidential mandates
✔ Proof that the activity happened (reports, summaries—classified if needed)
✔ Certification from the head of agency affirming authenticity

The law never allows: 
Fake names
Fictional persons
Joke names
Blank or unreadable signatures
"Non-existent individuals”
800 signatures without names
1,322 names with no PSA records

That is not confidentiality.

That is falsification under:

RA 3019 (Anti-Graft)
RA 6713 (Code of Conduct)
Art. 171 & 172 of the RPC (Falsification)

Pero seryoso, this is peak Philippine political absurdity in action: isang party-list na supposedly para sa mga construction workers na naghihirap sa araw-araw, pero ang representative pala ay may mansion sa Newport Beach, California na mas malaki pa sa buong barangay nila sa Mataasnakahoy. Imagine mo, yung taong nagsasabing “ipaglalaban ko ang mga manggagawa” tapos siya pala may car collection na pwede nang maging museum at real-estate portfolio na mas marami pa sa branches ng Jollibee sa SoCal.

Ang sakit sa puso kapag naisip mo na ang party-list system natin – na dapat talaga voice ng marginalized sectors – ay na-hijack na naman ng mga oligarchs at contractors. Classic “cong-tractor” move: pumasok ka bilang “representative ng masa,” tapos habang nakaupo ka sa Kongreso at nagdedeliberate ng budget, yung pamilya mo naman pala ang unang-unang nakakaalam kung saan pupunta ang bilyon-bilyong pondo ng DPWH. Pre-ordered pa raw ang projects – parang Shopee lang, add to cart agad bago pa ma-approve ang GAA! “Kailangan ko ‘yan for my constituents… and also for my pocket, char!”

At yung flood control anomaly? Perfect example ng irony: yung taong dapat nagpo-protekta sa mga tao laban sa baha, siya pa pala ang dahilan kung bakit patuloy na lumulubog ang Pilipinas – figuratively and literally.

Sa totoo lang, hindi na ako magugulat kung bukas may makita tayong bagong vlog title: “A Day in the Life of a Workers’ Rep: Morning hearing sa House, lunch sa yacht, afternoon site visit… sa California.” 😂

Pero beyond the chismis at meme potential, this case is depressing kasi pinapakita niya kung gaano ka-broken ang checks and balances natin. Kahit may COA red flags, tax evasion cases, defective projects, at literal plunder complaints, nakaka-upo pa rin at nakaka-jackpot pa rin ng bilyones. Kung ganito ang “representation” ng construction workers, baka mas maganda pang i-represent na lang sila ni Bob the Builder – at least siya honest na “Can we fix it? Yes we can!” hindi “Can we fix the bidding? Yes we can!”

Anyway, salamat sa detailed thread, OP. Sana magising na ang Ombudsman, Comelec, at buong bayan. Kasi kung hindi, next election baka may bagong party-list na: “Bilyonaryo Workers Solidarity” – requirements: SARILI mong crane, backhoe, at private helicopter. Qualified ka na agad. 😭🇵🇭
